title: Shortest Path: Routing Power for Minimum Loss
learningObjectives: Explain why BFS's hop-count shortest path isn't the same as the actual lowest-loss route on a weighted grid
Implement Dijkstra's algorithm to find the minimum-weight path between two nodes
Choose an appropriate edge weight (transmission loss, distance, capacity cost) for a given routing question
Recognize when Dijkstra's algorithm is not applicable (e.g. negative edge weights) and why
whyItMatters: BFS from the last lesson finds the path with the fewest transmission lines, but the fewest lines isn't necessarily the lowest-loss or cheapest route — a real grid operator cares about which path actually minimizes transmission loss or cost, and that requires weighting the edges by something meaningful, not just counting hops.
capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's minimum-loss routing calculation — directly answering the mission's original example question, 'how do I reduce energy costs by routing more efficiently.'

problem.summary: Fewer hops doesn't mean lower loss on a weighted grid.
problem.content: BFS tells you substation-1 to substation-4 is 2 hops away via substation-2, but that route might have higher cumulative transmission loss than a 3-hop alternative via substation-3 and substation-5 with lower-loss lines.

motivation.summary: Minimum-loss routing directly answers one of the mission's founding questions.
motivation.content: This is the direct technical answer to one of the very first example questions this whole mission was scoped around: 'how do I reduce energy costs?' Minimum-loss routing is a genuine, concrete instance of that question, and shortest-path algorithms with the right edge weights are exactly the tool.

theory.summary: Dijkstra finds minimum-total-weight paths; weight choice determines what 'shortest' means.
theory.content: Dijkstra's algorithm finds the minimum-total-weight path from a source node to every other node (or a specific target), by repeatedly visiting the unvisited node with the smallest known distance so far and updating its neighbors' distances if a shorter path through it is found. Edge weights represent whatever quantity you're minimizing — transmission loss percentage, physical distance, or a cost figure — and the choice of weight changes what 'shortest' actually means for the specific question being asked. Dijkstra's algorithm requires non-negative edge weights; a negative weight can cause it to settle on a node's distance before a cheaper path through a later negative edge is discovered, producing an incorrect result — an important limitation to recognize before applying it to a new problem.

visualExplanation.summary: Dijkstra's frontier expands to settle on the true minimum-loss path, contrasted with BFS's fewer-hops route.
visualExplanation.content: A weighted grid graph shows transmission-loss percentages labeled on each edge, with Dijkstra's algorithm's frontier expanding outward and settling on the true minimum-loss path highlighted at the end, contrasted with BFS's fewer-hops-but-higher-loss alternative.

interactiveDemo.summary: Adjust edge weights and watch the computed shortest path change accordingly.
interactiveDemo.content: Adjust edge weights (loss percentages) on a live grid graph and watch Dijkstra's computed shortest path change accordingly, directly observing how weight choice determines the routing outcome.

guidedCoding.summary: Follow along: implement Dijkstra's algorithm using a priority queue.

independentCoding.summary: Apply Dijkstra with loss-percentage weights to find a real minimum-loss route.
independentCoding.content: Apply Dijkstra's algorithm to your grid topology using transmission-loss percentage as the edge weight, and report the minimum-loss path and total loss between two specific substations.

debugging.summary: Fix a Dijkstra implementation missing the stale-entry staleness check.
debugging.content: A Dijkstra implementation is missing the `if current_dist > distances[node]: continue` staleness check, causing it to process outdated queue entries and potentially produce incorrect distances on a graph with many redundant paths. Identify and fix the missing check.

reflection.summary: Dijkstra can give a wrong answer with a negative edge weight.
reflection.content: Why would Dijkstra's algorithm give a wrong answer on a graph with a negative edge weight, even though the algorithm's logic looks like it should still 'find the smallest distance'?

teachBack.summary: Explain why fewest-hops and minimum-loss can genuinely differ.
teachBack.content: Explain to someone why the fewest-hops path from BFS and the minimum-loss path from Dijkstra can be genuinely different routes on the same grid, using your own topology's computed examples.

miniProject.summary: Compute minimum-loss routes for every substation pair and flag investment opportunities.
miniProject.content: Compute the minimum-loss routing path between every pair of substations in your grid topology, and identify which routes would most benefit from infrastructure investment to reduce loss further.

portfolioEvidence.summary: This is a direct, working answer to a founding question of the mission.
portfolioEvidence.content: This minimum-loss routing capability is a direct, working answer to one of the founding questions of the entire AI Energy OS mission.

labs.title: Dijkstra Routing
labs.description: Sandboxed exercise applying Dijkstra's algorithm with real loss-percentage weights across a grid topology, with a planted missing staleness-check bug to fix.

simulations.title: Dijkstra Weight Adjustment Visualizer
simulations.description: Live grid graph with adjustable edge weights, showing Dijkstra's computed shortest path change in real time.

flashcards.front: Why can the fewest-hops path (BFS) and the minimum-loss path (Dijkstra) be different routes on the same grid?
flashcards.back: BFS only counts the number of edges, ignoring their weights; Dijkstra minimizes the sum of edge weights (e.g. loss percentage), so a path with more hops but lower per-hop loss can beat a shorter-hop-count path with higher loss.

flashcards.front: Why does Dijkstra's algorithm require non-negative edge weights?
flashcards.back: It permanently settles on a node's shortest distance once visited, assuming no future path could be cheaper; a negative edge weight can violate that assumption, letting a cheaper path through a later negative edge go undiscovered.

rubric: Correctly diagnoses and fixes the missing staleness-check bug in the Dijkstra implementation
Computed minimum-loss routes are verified against the graph's actual weights, not just assumed correct
Correctly explains why negative edge weights break Dijkstra's core assumption
